basic_info.name: Polymer Membrane Gas Permeability
basic_info.name_ja: 高分子膜ガス透過性予測
basic_info.description: HSP distance between gas molecules (O2, N2, CO2, CH4, H2) and membrane polymer is used to predict gas permeability coefficients and ideal selectivity ratios. The approach is based on the solution-diffusion model: gas permeability P = S x D, where solubility S correlates with HSP affinity between gas and polymer, while diffusivity D relates to polymer free volume and chain mobility. Smaller HSP distance between a gas and the polymer matrix indicates higher solubility of that gas, enabling prediction of permeability ranking and membrane material selection for gas separation applications such as CO2 capture, O2/N2 enrichment, and natural gas sweetening.

hsp_principle.hsp_principle: Gas permeation through dense polymer membranes follows the solution-diffusion mechanism. The solubility coefficient S of a gas in a polymer correlates with the thermodynamic affinity between the gas and polymer, which can be quantified by the HSP distance Ra. A small Ra(gas-polymer) implies favorable gas-polymer interactions and higher solubility. For condensable gases (CO2, hydrocarbons), the HSP approach is particularly effective because solubility dominates permeability. The Flory-Huggins interaction parameter chi, estimable from Ra, directly relates to the Henry's law solubility coefficient. Gas selectivity (alpha_A/B = P_A/P_B) can be predicted by comparing Ra values: if Ra(A-polymer) < Ra(B-polymer), gas A will generally have higher solubility and thus higher permeability (for gases of similar size). However, size-sieving (diffusivity selectivity) must also be considered, especially for glassy polymers.
hsp_principle.key_equation: Ra = sqrt(4*(dD_gas - dD_polymer)^2 + (dP_gas - dP_polymer)^2 + (dH_gas - dH_polymer)^2); ln(S) ~ -Ra^2 * V_gas / (R * T); Selectivity alpha_A/B = (S_A/S_B) * (D_A/D_B)
hsp_principle.typical_red_threshold: No universal RED threshold; Ra < 6 MPa^0.5 generally indicates significant gas solubility in the polymer

required_inputs: HSP of target gas molecules (delta_d, delta_p, delta_h)
HSP of membrane polymer (delta_d, delta_p, delta_h)
Molar volume of gas (condensed or effective)
Polymer free volume fraction (for diffusivity estimation)
data_sources: Published gas HSP values (Yampolskii, Freeman compilations)
Group contribution methods for novel polymer HSP
NIST Webbook for gas physical properties
Experimental IGC data for polymer HSP

accuracy.limitations: HSP-based prediction primarily addresses the solubility component of permeability and does not fully capture diffusivity, which depends on polymer chain packing, free volume distribution, and gas molecular size. For light gases (H2, He) in glassy polymers, diffusivity selectivity dominates over solubility selectivity, limiting HSP predictive power. Gas HSP values are less well-established than liquid/polymer HSP, particularly for non-condensable gases where the concept of cohesive energy density is less straightforward. Plasticization by CO2 or hydrocarbons at high pressures changes polymer HSP in situ. Polymer aging (physical aging of glassy membranes) alters free volume over time, affecting permeability in ways not captured by static HSP.
known_failure_modes: Light gas (H2, He) permeation in glassy polymers where diffusivity selectivity dominates
CO2-induced plasticization at high pressures altering polymer matrix properties
Mixed gas effects where competitive sorption changes selectivity from ideal predictions
Highly crystalline or semi-crystalline polymers where gas transport occurs only through amorphous regions
Thin-film composite membranes where interfacial effects dominate
accuracy.validation_method: Constant-volume variable-pressure permeation testing (time-lag method), gravimetric or barometric sorption measurements for solubility coefficients, pure and mixed gas permeation experiments at controlled temperature and pressure

key_references.author: Yampolskii, Y.; Pinnau, I.; Freeman, B.D.
key_references.title: Materials Science of Membranes for Gas and Vapor Separation

key_references.author: Hansen, C.M.
key_references.title: Hansen Solubility Parameters: A User's Handbook, 2nd Edition

key_references.author: Lindvig, T.; Michelsen, M.L.; Kontogeorgis, G.M.
key_references.title: A Flory-Huggins model based on the Hansen solubility parameters

literature.regulatory_relevance: No direct regulatory mandates, but HSP-based membrane screening supports material selection for CO2 capture systems relevant to EPA greenhouse gas regulations and EU Emissions Trading System. Membrane materials for food-contact packaging (FDA, EU 10/2011) can use HSP screening to predict barrier properties.

commercial_tools: Materials Studio (molecular dynamics simulation of gas transport)
